one_line_takeaway: A training-free LLM text watermark that steers token-rank selection along a sinusoidal pattern and detects it with FFT, reaching AUC 0.97 vs GPTZero's 0.64 and surviving up to 60% token substitution/paraphrase - but only at temperature 0 and only for a model provider that controls its own generation.
key_finding: On the balanced human-written vs. watermarked-text task (Table I), the Signal Watermark detector reached AUC 0.97 (FPR 0.04, FNR 0.00) with OPT-1.3b and 0.84 (FPR 0.24, FNR 0.08) with text-davinci-003, versus GPTZero at AUC 0.64 (FNR 0.86) and DetectGPT at AUC 0.53 (FNR 0.96) - the baselines' very high FNR shows they mislabel watermarked model text as human because its perplexity is close to human text. Watermark-vs-non-watermark sensitivity (Table II) reached AUC 1.00 for OPT-1.3b and 0.88 for text-davinci-003. Multiple distinct watermarks (sin(x), sin(2x), sin(3x)) were distinguished with accuracy 0.87-0.98 (Table III). The watermark survived substitution and paraphrasing of up to 60% of tokens and, under Copy-Paste attack (Table IV), achieved recall 0.98-0.99 (precision 0.65 for OPT-1.3b, 0.84 for text-davinci-003). Detection works on spans as short as 10 tokens given sufficient context, and cross-model detection is best when the same model embeds and detects. Applying Nyquist's theorem, they argue up to 50 unique watermarks (sin(x) to sin(50x)) can be embedded at 10 tokens/cycle.
core_contribution: The paper proposes 'Signal Watermark', a scheme for embedding and detecting a watermark in LLM-generated text by imposing a pre-defined periodic (sinusoidal) signal on the token-rank selection process during generation. Instead of always sampling the top-ranked token, the method samples a sinusoidal pattern (e.g., sin(nx+phi)) scaled to a small integer range (e.g., 1-5) and, at each generation step, selects the candidate token whose rank within the sorted top-5 candidate pool matches the pattern value (selected_token_i = candidate_set_i[pattern[i]-1]). Detection is done via 'Token Probability Re-computation' (re-feeding the text token-by-token at temperature 0 to recover each token's rank within its candidate pool) followed by a Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) on the resulting rank series; a peak frequency matching the pre-defined pattern's frequency confirms the watermark. The authors' own claimed novelty is: (1) empirically validating the hypothesis that LLMs preserve consistent token-ranking patterns between generation and re-computation; (2) the signal-pattern embedding technique; (3) applying digital-signal-processing (FFT) to watermark detection; and (4) doing so with no additional model training and only API-level access. The many other watermarking methods (Kirchenbauer et al., CATER, DRW, GINSEW, AWT, EmbMarker, etc.) are described only in Related Work and are NOT this paper's contribution.
threat_model: Authentication/provenance of model-generated text: the defender wants to reliably determine whether a piece of text was produced by a specific LLM (to counter fake news, media rumors, and academic misuse), overcoming the weakness of perplexity/burstiness-based detectors that fail as LLM text approaches human quality. The adversary is a user who wants to pass off or launder watermarked LLM text, and who may try to erase the embedded signal. The paper evaluates four removal/evasion scenarios: (1) Copy-Paste attack (concatenating human prompt text with watermarked text, measured by per-token precision/recall via a 10-token sliding window), (2) Substitution attack (t5-large replaces 10%-60% of tokens), (3) Paraphrase attack (t5-large paraphrasing of increasing spans), and (4) Cross-Watermarking/Detecting where a different model is used for detection than for embedding.
limitations: Reported by the authors: watermarked text can contain minor grammatical inaccuracies and redundancy (a side effect of suppressing EOS tokens), lowering text quality; watermarked text has consistently higher perplexity than non-watermarked text at both temperature 0 and 0.8 (Appendix). Crucially, detection accuracy drops and perplexity rises as temperature increases, yet all headline results use temperature 0, whereas the authors note real deployments (e.g., web ChatGPT) run at ~0.7-0.9 - handling higher temperature is left to future work. Detection depends on the same-model re-computation and on enough left context (text-davinci-003 needs >=20 prompt tokens, OPT-1.3b ~25), and cross-model detection degrades. Reviewer-observed caveats: precision under Copy-Paste is modest (0.65 for OPT-1.3b); the embedding requires white-box or API access that exposes the top-k candidate log-probabilities and control of token selection, so it protects only a cooperating model provider's own outputs and does not detect arbitrary third-party LLM text; and evaluation is limited to three older models on a single news-style corpus.

datasets_used: C4 Realnewslike subset (Raffel et al.), 15G subset; ~3000 samples of >=250 tokens, generations standardized to 200 tokens with EOS suppressed
~400 watermarked samples for the multiple-watermark experiment (sin(x)/sin(2x)/sin(3x))
100 generated instances per text-length setting for the token-length study
quantitative_results: Human vs watermarked (Table I): OPT-1.3b AUC 0.97, FPR 0.04, FNR 0.00
Human vs watermarked (Table I): text-davinci-003 AUC 0.84, FPR 0.24, FNR 0.08
Baseline GPTZero (Table I): AUC 0.64, FPR 0.05, FNR 0.86
Baseline DetectGPT (Table I): AUC 0.53, FPR 0.04, FNR 0.96
Watermark vs non-watermark sensitivity (Table II): OPT-1.3b AUC 1.00 (FPR 0.00, FNR 0.00); text-davinci-003 AUC 0.88 (FPR 0.24, FNR 0.12)
Multiple-watermark accuracy (Table III): OPT-1.3b 0.92/0.98/0.96 for sin(x)/sin(2x)/sin(3x); text-davinci-003 0.87/0.89/0.92
Copy-Paste attack (Table IV): OPT-1.3b precision 0.65 / recall 0.98; text-davinci-003 precision 0.84 / recall 0.99
Robust to substitution and paraphrasing of up to 60% of tokens (still detectable)
Detectable in spans as short as 10 tokens; context needed: text-davinci-003 >=20 prompt tokens, OPT-1.3b ~25
Nyquist-based capacity claim: up to 50 unique watermarks (sin(x)..sin(50x)) at 10 tokens/cycle
Default config: temperature 0, amplitude range 1-5, 10 tokens per signal cycle
baselines_compared: DetectGPT (Mitchell et al., zero-shot probability-curvature detection)
